skill.name: code-review
skill.description: Reviews code changes for security issues, maintainability problems, performance risks, and test gaps with prioritized feedback.
skill.summary: Prioritized code review guidance for security, quality, performance, and maintainability.
skill.author: Joseph OBrien

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found the sensitive data, weak cryptography, and shell indicators are mostly markdown examples, placeholders, or checklist text. The remaining confirmed risk is legitimate external command guidance for git diff, which is read-only but should be disclosed because it can inspect repository content.

medium_findings.title: External Git Command Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to run git diff with command substitution to inspect branch changes. This is a real external command pattern, but it is a standard read-only code review workflow and does not show destructive commands, network access, or exfiltration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicitly present and would execute in the user repository. Confidence is not higher because the command is read-only and aligned with the stated code review purpose.

low_findings.title: Static Secret and Environment Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The API key and process.env references appear inside an illustrative markdown review example that teaches users to move secrets into environment variables. No evidence found of secret collection, runtime access, or network transmission.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious strings are contained inside a fenced example output, not executable skill logic. The surrounding text labels the value as an example of an exposed key and shows a remediation pattern.

low_findings.title: Template Backtick Findings Are Markdown Placeholders
low_findings.description: Several backtick and command-looking detections occur in the code analysis report template. The content is placeholder text for future reports, not executable Ruby, shell, or JavaScript logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are fenced markdown template sections with double-brace placeholders. No concrete command implementation or execution wrapper is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections map to generic security, naming, design pattern, and checklist language. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar cryptographic implementation in the cited files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found review prose and checklist items only. The files do not contain cryptographic code or instructions to use weak algorithms.

dangerous_patterns.title: Read-Only External Command Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends running git diff to inspect changes. This is useful for reviews, but it grants the agent visibility into repository changes and should be disclosed to users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present and operational, but its intent is transparent and limited to local Git inspection.

content.user_title: Review Code for Quality and Security
content.value_statement: Code reviews can miss security flaws, fragile logic, and maintainability issues under time pressure. This skill provides a structured review process with prioritized findings and actionable fixes.
actual_capabilities: Reviews git diffs and changed files to identify code quality issues.
Checks for common security problems such as exposed secrets, injection risks, and missing authorization.
Organizes feedback into critical issues, warnings, and suggestions.
Provides file and line references when reviewing concrete code.
Uses reference checklists for security, performance, testing, documentation, and maintainability.
limitations: It depends on the code and context available to the AI tool.
It may recommend running local Git commands to inspect changes.
It does not replace dedicated static analysis, dependency scanning, or penetration testing.
It cannot prove runtime behavior without tests, logs, or execution results.

use_cases.title: Pull Request Review
use_cases.description: Review changed files before merge and receive prioritized feedback on bugs, security, tests, and maintainability.

use_cases.title: Security-Focused Audit
use_cases.description: Check authentication, authorization, input handling, secrets, and data protection concerns in application code.

use_cases.title: Team Quality Standards
use_cases.description: Apply a consistent checklist when setting review expectations for code quality, documentation, and testing.

prompt_templates.title: Review Recent Changes
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of the current branch
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the recent code changes in this branch. Focus on correctness, security, maintainability, and missing tests.

prompt_templates.title: Review One File
prompt_templates.scenario: Targeted review of a specific file
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this file for security vulnerabilities, code quality issues, and performance risks. Prioritize the most important findings first.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Pull Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this pull request as a senior engineer. Separate must-fix issues from warnings and suggestions, and include clear remediation guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Security Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Conduct a security-focused code review of these changes. Check authentication, authorization, input validation, secrets handling, dependency risk, error handling, and test coverage.

output_examples.input: Review a modified authentication route before merge.
output: Critical: The route accepts user input without validation before authentication logic.
Warning: Error responses reveal too much detail about account state.
Suggestion: Add tests for expired tokens and malformed credentials.

output_examples.input: Review recent changes across five files.
output: Summary: No critical issues found, but three warnings require attention before merge.
Warning: Shared formatting logic is duplicated across two modules.
Suggestion: Improve variable names in the list rendering component.

output_examples.input: Run a security checklist against a new API endpoint.
output: Critical: Authorization checks are missing for protected records.
Warning: Request size limits are not enforced.
Suggestion: Document expected error responses for client developers.

best_practices: Provide the relevant diff, file paths, and intended behavior before asking for review.
Ask for severity-ranked findings so critical issues are easy to act on first.
Run project tests and scanners separately when the review identifies risky changes.
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill as the only security gate for high-risk production systems.
Do not ask for broad repository review when a focused diff is available.
Do not accept suggested fixes without checking project conventions and tests.

faq.question: What does this skill review?
faq.answer: It reviews code changes for correctness, security, maintainability, performance, tests, documentation, and project conventions.

faq.question: Does it run commands?
faq.answer: It may recommend read-only Git commands, such as git diff, to inspect changed files when the tool environment allows it.

faq.question: Can it find security vulnerabilities?
faq.answer: It can identify common vulnerability patterns, but it should be paired with scanners and human review for critical systems.

faq.question: Does it support pull request review?
faq.answer: Yes. It is designed to review pull requests and organize feedback by priority.

faq.question: Will it modify code automatically?
faq.answer: The skill focuses on review feedback. Code changes depend on the user prompt and the capabilities of the host AI tool.

faq.question: What information should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide the diff, changed files, project context, expected behavior, and any specific risks you want reviewed.
